title: TPU v1 · MAC Datapath RTL: Growing 8b × 8b into a 32b Signed Accumulation
oneLine: Correct MAC RTL depends on width and signedness. Two 8-bit operands need a 16-bit product, a 256-term dot product needs a wider accumulator, and every stage from input through accumulation must preserve signed two's-complement semantics. One missing signed declaration can reinterpret the sign bit as magnitude.

text-anchor="start">Rule: multiply grows width; addition/accumulation needs headroom; keep every arithmetic stage signed</text></g></svg>

legend.name: 8-bit signed inputs (a, b)
legend.desc: Activation a and weight b are INT8. In RTL, logic signed [7:0] a, b declares two's-complement operands. Without signed, synthesis treats -1 as unsigned 255 and arithmetic is wrong.

legend.name: Multiplier (8b × 8b → 16b)
legend.desc: The * operator infers multiplier hardware. Two 8-bit operands require a 16-bit signed product, so prod is declared signed [15:0]. This is the first width expansion.

legend.name: 32-bit signed accumulator
legend.desc: Summing 256 signed products requires more than the 16-bit product width; TPU v1 uses 32-bit accumulation. The clocked acc <= acc + prod update preserves the partial sum, and insufficient width would silently wrap on overflow.

legend.name: Width-growth budget
legend.desc: Datapath widths must be derived: multiplication adds operand widths, addition needs carry/sign headroom, and repeated accumulation needs log2(depth) growth plus margin. Explicit sizing prevents truncation and overflow.

steps.cap: ① Declare a and b as 8-bit signed operands: logic signed [7:0] a, b. The signed qualifier determines two's-complement rather than unsigned multiplication.
② Write assign prod = a * b. The * operator infers a multiplier. Declare prod as signed [15:0] because an 8b × 8b full-precision result needs 16 bits.
③ Accumulate with acc <= acc + prod. Sign-extend the 16-bit product to 32 bits before addition. The accumulator is clocked state and therefore uses always_ff with nonblocking <=.
④ Derive the width budget: a 256-term dot product needs roughly 23 signed magnitude bits at the worst bound; 32 bits provides margin and avoids wraparound. Width is calculated, not guessed.
⑤ End-to-end: signed 8b inputs → signed 16b product → signed 32b accumulation. Once one PE is correct, 65,536 homogeneous PEs compose the 256 × 256 systolic array.

before: Define the language and coding style: use a synthesizable SystemVerilog/Verilog subset, always_ff with nonblocking <= for sequential state, always_comb with blocking = for combinational logic, and a consistent reset policy. These are standard practices; TPU source is not published line by line.

Calculate each width: INT8 inputs produce a 16-bit product. Accumulating 256 terms needs about 23 bits at the mathematical bound, so a 32-bit accumulator provides margin and matches the disclosed TPU accumulator precision.
Define signed and unsigned domains: INT8 inference uses signed two's-complement values. Specify a, b, prod, and acc as signed and document every deliberate unsigned control or address signal.

Freeze the PE interface: define widths and directions for activation, resident weight, partial-sum input/output, clock, reset, and enable before replicating the PE 65,536 times. One interface error multiplies across the array.
Constrain RTL to synthesizable intent: use * and + and let synthesis map arithmetic; do not hand-code gates or rely on simulation-only delay or initial behavior.
during: Operators infer hardware: a * b becomes a multiplier and acc + prod becomes an adder. Datapath RTL is an explicitly sized arithmetic graph; synthesis implements the operators.

Width rule: multiply expands, add preserves carry, accumulate for depth. An 8b × 8b product needs 16 bits; addition needs sign/carry headroom; 256 terms require a wider accumulator. Too few bits silently truncate the high end and wrap around.

Declare signedness at every stage: logic signed [7:0] a,b; logic signed [15:0] prod; logic signed [31:0] acc; (illustrative). If one intermediate becomes unsigned, negative weights and extension behavior can be corrupted while waveforms still show plausible bit patterns.

Make sign extension explicit and reviewable: adding a 16-bit product into a 32-bit accumulator requires sign extension, not zero extension. Fully signed expressions normally extend correctly, but use explicit casts such as $signed() at mixed-type boundaries.

Separate combinational and sequential behavior: multiply/add logic is combinational; acc is a register updated in always_ff @(posedge clk) with <=. An illustrative one-MAC-per-cycle form is always_ff @(posedge clk) if(en) acc <= acc + a*b;

Keep the evidence boundary clear: the exact TPU v1 RTL is not public. Signedness, width, and always_ff examples follow standard datapath RTL practice; disclosed facts are the 8-bit MACs, 32-bit accumulators, 256 × 256 array, and INT8 throughput.

result.label: Reuse
result.value: Replicate one PE 65,536 times → 256 × 256 systolic array

after: Run width and signedness lint: treat width mismatch, signed/unsigned mixing, and implicit truncation as real defects. Many low-level datapath bugs can be stopped before simulation.
Simulate numerical boundaries: test -128, +127, all-negative weights, and vectors near the 256-term accumulation bound. Compare random stimulus against a bit-accurate golden model.
Run trial synthesis: verify that * and + map to the intended multiplier and adder widths, no unexpected truncation occurs, and PE timing/area is compatible with 700 MHz.

Check CDC at domain boundaries: the PE is synchronous to the array clock, but interfaces to PCIe and DDR3 may use other clocks. Verify synchronized control and safe data transport at the array boundary.
Hand off the numerical contract: provide RTL, the width budget, signedness rules, and a bit-accurate model to verification. Freeze interface widths before synthesis and physical implementation.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (8-bit MACs, 32-bit accumulators, and the 256 × 256 array are disclosed TPU v1 facts; RTL signedness and coding examples reflect standard practice)
